title: Current-driven reduction of topological protection in multichannel superconductors
abstract: We investigate the robustness of topological phases in a Kitaev ladder composed of two coupled superconducting chains under the perturbing influence of a finite charge current. By introducing an effective Hamiltonian depending on the quasiparticle momentum induced by the current, we show that the two-mode topological phase, present in the isolated ladder, is fragile against a finite current flux. To characterize this behavior, we combine bulk topological invariants with real-space diagnostics, including the edge-edge quantum conditional mutual information Iee, which provides an entanglement-based signature of topological order. Our results provide an effective framework to describe how current injection and measurement processes can affect topological protection in superconducting nanostructures.
summary: This paper demonstrates that injecting a charge current into a multichannel superconductor breaks time-reversal and chiral symmetries, which suppresses higher-order topological phases. It identifies that while the two-mode phase is fragile under current, it can be stabilized if the superconducting channels remain decoupled.
main_problem: Investigating how a finite charge current induces symmetry breaking that reduces the topological protection and suppresses higher-multiplicity Majorana modes in multichannel superconductors.
main_result: The study shows that a finite current drives the system from the BDI class to the D class, making the two-mode topological phase fragile, unless the channels are decoupled (interchain pairing is zero).
method: The authors use a real-space Hamiltonian approach, combining bulk topological invariants (Pfaffian) with real-space entanglement diagnostics like edge-edge quantum conditional mutual information (Iee).
model_or_system: A Kitaev ladder model consisting of two coupled spinless p-wave superconducting chains with longitudinal and interchain hopping and pairing.
key_observables: Topological invariants (Z and Z2), Bogoli-de Gennes (BdG) energy spectra, Majorana Bound State (MBS) probability densities, and edge-edge quantum conditional mutual information (Iee).
important_parameters: Chemical potential (mu), longitudinal hopping (t), interchain hopping (t1), longitudinal pairing (Delta), interchain pairing (Delta1), and current-induced momentum (q).
main_assumptions: The current is modeled via an effective Hamiltonian using a Peierls-like substitution (q-dependent phase factor).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows phase diagrams comparing equilibrium and current-carrying regimes; Figure 2 shows energy spectra and mode densities illustrating the lifting of the two-mode configuration; Figure 3 shows the persistence of the two-mode phase when interchain pairing is zero; Figure 4 shows energy spectra as a function of chemical potential and interchain hopping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of current-induced symmetry breaking, defines the Kitaev ladder model and current-carrying Hamiltonian, presents bulk and real-space topological analysis, discusses the transition from BDI to D class, and concludes with the implications for superconducting nanostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a),(b): phase diagrams of the Kitaev ladder for ∆1 = 0.5, evaluated at q = 0 and q = 0.6, respectively. The color scale labels the corresponding topological sectors. Panels (c)–(h) display the probability densities of the lowest-energy BdG modes for representative points selected from the phase diagrams. The real-space calculations are performed on a ladder composed of two chains, each of length L = 100. The corresponding BdG energies of the modes are reported in the insets.

FIG. 2. Panels (a) and (b) show horizontal cuts of the phase diagrams along the red lines indicated in Fig. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively, while panels (c) and (d) show the corresponding vertical cuts. In all panels, the four BdG eigenvalues closest to zero are plotted for a ladder composed of two chains, each of length L = 100. The spectra are shown both in the absence of current, q = 0, in panels (a) and (c), and in the current-carrying regime, q = 0.6, in panels (b) and (d). The interchain pairing is fixed to ∆1 = 0.5 in all panels.

FIG. 3. Panels (a), (c), and (e): phase diagrams obtained from the topological invariant in k-space introduced in Sec. II and for increasing values of the current flux q = 0, 0.3, and 0.6, respectively. Panels (b), (d), and (f): corresponding phase diagrams obtained from the edge–edge quantum conditional mutual information Iee. The agreement between the two sets of panels highlights the consistency between the momentum-space invariant and the real-space entanglement-based characterization. The entanglement calculations are performed on a ladder composed of two chains, each of length L = 50, with edge sizes fixed to LA = LB = 12 sites per chain.

FIG. 4. Panel (a) shows a horizontal cut, while panel (b) shows a vertical cut of the phase diagrams obtained from the edge–edge quantum conditional mutual information Iee in Fig. 3. The cuts are taken at fixed t1 = 0.7 in panel (a) and at fixed µ = 1.2 in panel (b). The dashed lines indicate the ideal quantized values corresponding to the different topological sectors. Deviations from these quantized plateaus become more pronounced as the current flux q increases, reflecting the growing relevance of finite-size effects.

FIG. 5. Panel (a) shows the phase diagram of Fig. 3 for ∆1 = 0 and q = 0.6, together with the horizontal and vertical cuts used to compute the spectra displayed in panels (b) and (c). Panel (b) shows the spectrum as a function of µ at fixed t1 = 0.3, while panel (c) shows the spectrum as a function of t1 at fixed µ = 0.7. In both cases, the four BdG eigenvalues closest to zero energy are plotted for a ladder composed of two chains, each of length L = 100. The agreement between the gap closings and the phase boundaries confirms the bulk– edge correspondence.
